coredata.dc:title: Improving mechanical and thermal property of pure copper matrix simultaneously by carbonized polymer dots (CPD) cluster reinforcement
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Wenmin
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Fang, Dong
Li, Caiju
Tao, Jingmei
Li, Fengxian
Liu, Yichun
You, Xin
Tan, Songlin
coredata.dc:description: A novel carbonized polymer dots (CPD)/copper (Cu) composite was successfully synthesized via molecular level mixing (MLM) and spark plasma sintering (SPS) process. Microstructure analysis shows that the CPD clusters are well dispersed at the Cu grain interfaces by forming an amorphous carbon (AC) network with a large number of inserted graphene carbon dots (some of them show the typical graphene microstructure). Mechanical properties of the CPD/Cu composite are remarkably higher than that of pure Cu sample, which increases initially with CPD wt.% and then decreases. 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu composite obtains ~47% higher strength than that of pure Cu matrix. Meanwhile, the thermal conductivity is improved from 305 W/m·K to 360 W/m·K without any adverse impact on the mechanical properties. This study provides new insights into the preparation of the advanced Cu composite with enhanced comprehensive performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots (CPD)
CPD/Cu composite
Amorphous carbon (AC) network
Mechanical property
Thermal conductivity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Chinese National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 52064032 ), the Yunnan science and technology projects Grants (Grant No. 2019ZE001 , No. 202002AB080001 ), Yunnan Ten Thousand Talents Plan Young & Elite Talents Project ( YNWR-QNBJ-2018-005 ). Project also supported by State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, China.

This work is supported by the Chinese National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 52064032), the Yunnan science and technology projects Grants (Grant No. 2019ZE001, No. 202002AB080001), Yunnan Ten Thousand Talents Plan Young & Elite Talents Project (YNWR-QNBJ-2018-005). Project also supported by State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the CPD/Cu composite preparation.
TEM and HRTEM image of (a) the CPD, (b) particle size statistic of the CPD, (c) The XPS full spectrum of the CPD, high-resolution of (d)C1s, (e–f) N1s, and O1s. (g) TGA and DGA curves.
(a–b) SEM image of the Cu2O and CPD/Cu2O, (c) FTIR spectra of the CPD, (d) XRD pattern of the CPD and CPD/Cu composite powders. (e–i) TEM and HRTEM of the 0.4 wt%CPD/Cu composite.

Tensile fractography of the composites. (a) Stress-strain curves of the prepared-CPD/Cu composites, (b) Cu, (c) 0.4 wt%CPD/Cu, (d) the magnified view of the yellow wireframe in (c), (e) after being bombarded by electrons for 5s of (d), (f) 0.8 wt%CPD/Cu composite. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Calculated and experimental value (left part) and contributions (right part) of the tensile strength the 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu composites.
(a) Thermal conductivity of Cu and CPD/Cu composites from 298 to 500K; (b, c, d) schematic diagrams of interface structure of CPD/Cu composite.

head.ce:title: Improving mechanical and thermal property of pure copper matrix simultaneously by carbonized polymer dots (CPD) cluster reinforcement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbonized polymer dots (CPD)/copper (Cu) composite was successfully synthesized via molecular level mixing (MLM) and spark plasma sintering (SPS) process. Microstructure analysis shows that the CPD clusters are well dispersed at the Cu grain interfaces by forming an amorphous carbon (AC) network with a large number of inserted graphene carbon dots (some of them show the typical graphene microstructure). Mechanical properties of the CPD/Cu composite are remarkably higher than that of pure Cu sample, which increases initially with CPD wt.% and then decreases. 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu composite obtains ~47% higher strength than that of pure Cu matrix. Meanwhile, the thermal conductivity is improved from 305 W/m·K to 360 W/m·K without any adverse impact on the mechanical properties. This study provides new insights into the preparation of the advanced Cu composite with enhanced comprehensive performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene (GR) and derivatives of GR, as reinforcements, are widely employed in preparation of high-performance copper matrix composites [1]. However, it comes to the fact that dispersity of the reinforcement and interfacial combination between the reinforcement and matrix is still a huge challenge for the composite fabrication [2]. A good balance between mechanical [3–7] and thermal property [8–12] for the composites is very difficult to reach. In most cases, increase of the mechanical property is often at the expense of thermal property. Take reduced graphene oxide (RGO) reinforcing pure copper as an example, tensile strengthen increased from 186 to 292 MPa, whereas thermal conduction is reduced from 350 to 330 W/m·K in previous report [9]. The same situation can be found in many researches [10,13–15]. The fundamental reason is that some results like grain refinement and dislocation increment in the RGO/Cu composite are only beneficial to improve the mechanical properties, but not to enhance the thermal conduction owing to the interface scattering effect [13].

Carbonized Polymer Dots (CPD), which are also referred as graphene/graphene oxide quantum dots (GQD/GOQD), carbon quantum dots, carbon nanodots and carbon dots (CD) etc, are an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials with various unique properties. They are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles with amorphous to nanocrystalline cores that have diameters in the range of 3–10 nm [16]. Very different from the other carbon nanomaterials, abundant epoxy, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups exist on the base and surface of CPD. Due to the existence of these functional groups, the water solubility of CPD is excellent, and it can be further functionalized and surface passivated, the dispersion problem of CPD in copper matrix can be better resolved by a suitable method [17,18]. More importantly, amorphous carbon at the shell network is beneficial to improve the interface bonding between the reinforcement and metal matrix, which is extremely important from the point of theory and technology [19]. It brings the hope and dawn to solve the above hard problems of dispersion and interface bonding for designing and preparing a composite with superior performance.

To our certain knowledge, however, the reports about CPD enhancements in metal composites are very limited. The aim of this study is to resolve the dilemma of achieving good dispersity and intimate contact of nano-carbon materials with copper matrix. A combination of molecular level mixing (MLM) and spark plasma sintering (SPS) is employed for preparing the CPD reinforced pure Cu composite. The influence and mechanism of CPD on mechanical and thermal conductivity properties of copper matrix are examined in details. This work provides a new idea and approach for the preparation of advanced Cu matrix composites.

Synthesis and characterization of CPD are exhaustively reported in previous papers [16]. Briefly, our preparative procedure was consisted of reaction and freeze-dry. Citric acid (6 g) and Ethylene diamine (2 mL) were added to distilled water (60 mL). The solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (80 mL) and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. After fully reaction, the reactors were cooled down to room temperature naturally. Then the solution, which showed a brown-black and transparent color, was subjected to dialysis for 48 h to obtain a more purer CPD sample. Finally, this colloidal solution was freeze-dried for 24 h to achieve CPD powder reinforcement (see Fig. 1 ).

CPD/Cu composite was prepared from powder preparation by MLM to sintering by SPS. The as-received CPD (diameter range: 1–4 nm, average: 2.20 nm) was dispersed in deionized water. Subsequently, the MLM of CPD and Cu acetate monohydrate (CuAc) was performed to form a micro-sized CPD/Cu2O composite powder. The content of CPD clusters in the composite powders is 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8 wt%, respectively. Then, CPD/Cu composite powders were obtained by being heated in flowing H2–N2 (5 vol% H2) atmosphere at 573 K for 6 h. Finally, CPD/Cu composite powder was consolidated by SPS at 1023 K for 10 min under a pressure of 50 MPa.

Microscopic morphology and structure of CPD were analyzed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2-TF30, S-Twin), atomic force microscopy (BRUKER Dimension Icon, AFM) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha+, Thermo fisher Scientific). TGA and DTG are tested in Ar atmosphere from 25 to 800 °C at a rate of 8 °C per minute. Microscopic morphology, functional groups of Cu2O, CPD/Cu2O, CPD were studied by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Nova Namo-450, FEI) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet is 10). The phase of the composite powder was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, RINT, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. The interfacial microstructure of CPD/Cu composites was studied by TEM. The density of composites was evaluated according to the principle of Archimedes. Tensile test was conducted by an AG-IS 10 KN equipment with a crosshead speed of 0.2 mm/min at room temperature. The fracture surface of CPD/Cu composites was analyzed by SEM. Finally, the electrical conductivity and thermal conductivity of CPD/Cu composites were measured by digital conductivity meter (sigma 2008B, Xiamen Tianyan Instrument Co. LTD, China) and laser thermal conductivity analyzer (NETZSCH LFA 427, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CPD and CPD/Cu composite powders
ce:section.ce:para: Structure and morphology of the as-prepared CPD and composite powders are firstly investigated as shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2(a) indicates that the obtained CPD are approximately spherical, and its average particle diameter is about 2.20 nm (in Fig. 2(b)). Moreover, part of CPD is well-formed and the interplanar spacing is about 0.21 nm, which is a typical graphite phase (100) crystal plane [20], the other part does not have a complete crystal structure, showing an amorphous structural characteristic. AFM results confirm that a uniform distribution of nanoparticles with a topographic height ranging from 0.2 nm to 2.0 nm, corresponding to 1–4 graphene-like network stacking in the cores (see Fig. S1) [21]. XPS spectrum clearly reveals that carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are presented in the CPD (in Fig. 2(c)), the composition of 61.23% carbon, 31.11% oxygen and 7.66% nitrogen element suggest that CPD are carbon-rich material. In the expanded XPS spectra, the C1s peaks at 284.19, 284.97, 286.39, 288.16, and 288.98 eV are shown in Fig. 2(d), which can be ascribed to carbon in the form of C =C (sp2), C–C (sp3), C–N/C–O, CO/CN, and O–CO, respectively [22,23]. The ratio of sp2 (11.76%) and sp3 (36.96%) is 7/22, indicating that the synthesized CPD contain lots of defects. The N1s peaks at 398.37, 399.19, 399.84, and 400.2 eV as shown in Fig. 2(e) indicate that nitrogen exists mostly in the form of CN–C, C–N–C, C-NHx, and N–C3, respectively [24,25]. The O1s peaks at 530.57, 531.18, and 532.28 eV in Fig. 2(f) are associated with O–CO, C–O, and CO, respectively. The characterization results from XPS suggest that the surface of the as-synthesized CPD is functionalized by multiple oxygen containing and nitrogen functional groups. Moreover, polymer features and chemical structure of the C6H8O7·H2O and CPD are investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) (in Fig. 2(g)). The DTG curve presents the weight loss rate, and the peaks in the TGA curve are used to estimate the degree of thermal degradation. Pure C6H8O7·H2O compound starts to melt at ~150 °C and decompose at ~180 °C, but CPD powder exhibits four-step decomposition process, which is like polymers, implying the CPD contains the polymer chains. Different from classical polymers, four-stage degradation of the CPD includes as follows: the first degradation at ~120 °C is assigned to desorption from absorbed H2O molecules; the second thermal degradation step mainly corresponded to the decomposition of the unreacted C6H8O7·H2O raw material with peak temperature at ~200 °C; the three-thermal degradation at ~300 °C is ascribed to decomposition from groups (amide, hydroxyl, unsaturated double bonds, ether bonds, etc.) on polymer chains of the CPD; and, the last degradation at ~540 °C describes the pyrolysis of inner cross-linked polymer chains and polymer network. The final residual weight is ~20% over 600 °C thermal degradation, indicates the weight percentage of carbon cores in the CPD [26]. TGA and DTG results support the existing microstructure of polymer chains and carbon cores in the CPD.

Morphology of the Cu2O and 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu2O composite powder prepared by MLM is represented in Fig. 3 (a and b), respectively. Different from the smooth surface of the Cu2O with the size of ~300 nm, the surface of the CPD/Cu2O composite powder is obviously rough and the size of the Cu2O particle is increased to ~400 nm. This indicates that the CPD clusters may be mainly located on the Cu2O particle surface. FTIR spectra reveals that the surface of the CPD contains various kinds of functional groups, such as –OH, N–H (~3400 cm−1), –CH (~2918 cm−1), CO (~1700 cm−1, ~1600 cm−1, ~1425 cm−1), –NH (~1380 cm−1), C–N (~1209 cm−1), and C–NH–C (~1108 cm−1), which demonstrates that the hydrophilicity and stability of the CPD in aqueous system is improved. However, compared with the functional groups of the CPD, no C–N and C–NH–C functional groups are found in the CPD/Cu2O powder due to the combination of the CPD and Cu2O grain (in Fig. 3(c)). The CPD/Cu composite powder is obtained after reduction treatment. As revealed in Fig. 3(d), the phase structure and crystallinity of the CPD and CPD/Cu composite powder after reduction treatment are also analyzed by XRD. No characteristic peaks of the CPD powder located around 18° (characteristic peak of carbon) are detected in the CPD/Cu composite powder, only characteristic peaks of Cu crystal cell are found, which may be attributed to the low content of CPD in the CPD/Cu composite powders [27]. In addition, the synthesized CPD contain a great deal of amorphous carbon structure, which agrees with the results in Fig. 2. Fig. 3(e) shows the representative TEM image of the 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu composite. White spots with different sizes are clearly well scattered and distributed at the Cu grain boundary, which may be formed by polymerized the CPD clusters together. Some of the spots are enlarged and analyzed carefully as displayed in Fig. 3(f and g), we find that the CPD clusters are destroyed by varying degrees, which is most likely introduced during the TEM sample preparation by the ion beam thinning. A non-damaged spot is provided in Fig. 3(h), it states that CPD are uniformly dispersed on the CPD clusters, just like lots of graphene dot embedded in a huge AC-network. In addition, interestingly, during the TEM observation with 5 kV voltages, the AC-network is broking down by the electron beam bombardment. The interface of the CPD and Cu is analyzed by HRTEM in detail (see Fig. 3(i)), it is worth noting that there is a significant transition region between the AC-network and the Cu with a width of about ~5 nm. According to the fast Fourier transform diagram in Fig. 3(i) inset, the relationship of the Cu (111), CPD (100) crystal plane and amorphous carbon microstructure are revealed. Diffusion of the carbon atoms into the Cu crystal may happen due to the highly active amorphous carbon atoms and sintering temperature during the SPS process [28]. Herein, the transition region is desirable to improve the interface bonding of the CPD and Cu, which is a typical non-reactive metal-graphite system.

Mechanical properties of composites have been tested at least three times, as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a), high relative density of all the samples is reached with the same sintering process. Even the 0.4 wt%CPD/Cu composite sample (density: 98.5% ± 0.4) obtains higher densification than pure Cu sample (density: 95.0% ± 0.6). It also reveals that the ultra-tensile strength (UTS) of the CPD/Cu composites is greatly improved compared with that of the pure Cu sample. The addition of 0.4 wt%CPD, particularly, results in ~47% improvement in mechanical property, which is more significant than that of the previous research work [29]. Enhanced strength and less reduced elongation (EL: ~13.5%) are both beneficial to the practical structural application of the composites. In the Refs, the UTS of bulk Cu is 255–294 MPa obtained by MLM [30,31] and ~258 MPa by ball-milling [32], this difference is caused by the grain size and grain boundary with different preparation methods. It indicates that our results of pure copper sample are very close to those in the reports.

Fig. 4(b) (c) and (f) show the tensile fractography of the pure Cu, 0.4 wt%CPD/Cu and 0.8 wt%CPD/Cu composites after tensile deformation. By comparative analysis, more dimples are shown in the pure Cu sample, but the size and the depth are very different. The low magnification images infer many dimples, demonstrating the ductile fracture mode. Interestingly, for the 0.4 wt%CPD/Cu specimen, after being bombarded by an electron beam for 5 s at a voltage of 10 kV, the small particles in the dimples are gone gradually, as shown in the high magnification image (see Fig. 4(d and e)). It may be caused by the amorphous carbon which is ablated under the irradiation of an electron beam. This phenomenon is consistent with the microstructure of the CPD by HRTEM observation, which can also be used to better explain the disappearance of small particles in the dimple of Fig. 3(f and g). However, some clusters of small particles in the dimple can be observed in Fig. 4(f), which may be caused by a great quantity of existing CPD. The precipitation behavior at the dimples is very different from those of CNT or graphene in Ref. [5,33], because the CPD is not only composed of sp2-Carbon, but also lots of amorphous carbon, and the polymer which is not completely reacted on the surface can polymerizes together as a cluster.

Electrical conductivity of the composites at room temperature was measured using a digital conductivity meter. The electrical conductivity is slightly decreased from 85% IACS to 81% IACS for 0.2 wt%CPD/Cu composites, 82% IACS for 0.4 wt%CPD/Cu, 80% IACS for the 0.6 wt%CPD/Cu composites, and 78% IACS for the 0.8 wt%CPD/Cu composites, which may be attributed to the AC-network of CPD to affect the electrical conductivity.

Thermal conductivity of the composites was measured by laser thermal conductivity analyzer at five different temperatures (298K, 348K, 398K, 448K and 498K) as shown in Fig. 6(a). The obtained thermal conductivity of CPD/Cu composites varies from 305 W/m·K to 360 W/m·K, showing a strong temperature dependence. The thermal conductivity of the sintered Cu sample is much smaller than that of bulk single crystal Cu (404 W/m·K) due to the existence of grain boundaries and void defects as a polycrystalline material [43]. Obviously, the thermal conductivity of pure copper is less than that of the composite materials. The thermal conductivity of 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu composite, especially, is close to 360 W/m·K at 448K, shows an improvement of 14%. Besides, the thermal conductivity of all composites substantial increases firstly and then decreases slightly from room temperature to 498K. Even for 0.8 wt%CPD/Cu composite, the thermal conductivity is ~34 W/m·K higher than that of pure Cu at 498 K. However, for the 0.8 wt%CPD/Cu composite, the thermal conductivity starts to decrease owing to the increased pores and amorphous carbon ratio.

It is well accepted that high tensile properties of Cu matrix composite depend on many factors, such as full densification, uniform dispersion of reinforcement, strong interface between reinforcement and matrix, and so on [34]. Herein, pure Cu and 0.4 wt%CPD/Cu composite are taken as examples to better understand the main strengthening mechanism. From the TEM analysis results, the CPD clusters are mainly located at the grain boundary of the matrix, the increased strength of the CPD/Cu composite can be ascribed as initial dislocation strengthening △σD [35], grain refinement according to Hall-Patch relationship △σH-P [36], Orowan looping strengthening △σO [37], thermal mismatch strengthening △σT [38] and load transfer from matrix to reinforcements △σL [39]. All of them are intensively discussed in many researches. The related formula involved in this part are listed in the supplementary material. However, not all enhancement mechanisms have a significant contribution to the YS and UTS.

As grain statistics results shown in Fig. S2, the average grain size of the pure Cu is ~0.84 μm, and that of the 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu composite is ~0.83 μm, which indicates an almost negligible impact of the grain refinement of the composite. Because the employed SPS technique in this study enables to fully consolidate the composites with a very short holding time at a relatively low sintering temperature, which can suppress the grain growth during the densification process. Orowan looping strengthening also can be ignored because the CPD clusters are mainly distributed on the Cu grain boundary. Besides, it is well known that thermal mismatch reinforcement makes little contribution to the strength of SPS sintered composites and is only effective for hardened composites [40]. In conclusion, the strength of the composites can be predicted by the following equation:
(1)
Δ σ = Δ
σ D
+ Δ
σ L

In the case of dislocation placement, dislocation multiplication and annihilation will occur simultaneously in the tensile deformation of composite materials, and the whole deformation process will be dominated by the competition between the two processes [41]. In other words, the deformation of the CPD/Cu interface will be accompanied with dislocation multiplication and annihilation, thus maintaining more strain and higher strength [42]. Thus, the XRD analysis of bulk samples before and after tensile test was employed to quantitative estimate the dislocation density (see Fig. S3). It can be calculated by Equation S(2), the improved strength contributed by the dislocation strengthening of CPD is measured ~25.8 MPa.

Additionally, it points to the fact that the CPD clusters will hold a higher ability to block the sustained strain hardening in the CPD/Cu composites due to the enhanced interfacial bonding in CPD/Cu composites. Unlike CNT and graphene, the AC-network is produced without any destruction of the internal complete crystal structure which is a part of the original intact CPD, in fact, plenty of CPD with a complete crystal structure are uniformly dispersed in the AC-network. This indicates that the crystal structure part of the CPD is not destroyed during the sintering process, but the AC-network can be generated by the displacement and combination of a lot of disordered carbon atoms, which are introduced from the carbonization of the polymers, it is reasonable that the formation of AC-network combined with the analysis of TEM, XPS and XRD patterns of the CPD. Undoubtedly, this network is one of the key factors in evenly dispersed of CPD, and it is important in the bonding properties of the CPD and Cu grains. According to equation S(3) and S(3i), the value of △σL is 143.6 MPa for 0.4 wt%CPD/Cu composites (as shown in Fig. 5
). Attentionally, the strength value of the calculated composite is slightly higher than the experimental value, which is because the calculated value is close to the ideal state, whereas there are some uncontrollable factors in the experiment.

It is worth emphasizing that UTS of the 0.2 wt%CPD/Cu composite is 420 ± 12 MPa, which is as high as that of 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu. Both 0.2 wt% and 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu composites show a higher work-hardening rate during the tensile process. However, the elongation of 0.4 wt% CPD/Cu sample is much higher than that of 0.2 wt%CPD/Cu, which may be explained by the more CPD resulting in a more complete network structure, further preventing the crack initiation and propagation.

Residual and thermal components can be used to represent the thermal conductivity of metal material. In the study, dislocation, grain boundaries, pores and impurity atoms are classified as residuals, and these defects are almost independent of temperature [44]. Structural defects in composite materials or alloys will affect the movement of electrons [45]. The structure imperfections of the composites are reduced as mentioned in the previous section. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the CPD clusters are well-distributed at Cu grain boundaries. In the process of heat transfer, both the probability of electron hopping through defects and the number of free electrons may increase. The thermal conductivity of CPD/Cu composites is positively correlated with temperature because electrons rather than phonons have a dominant influence on thermal conductivity from room temperature to 773 K. In quantum mechanics, the only electrons that can participate in heat conduction are those the Fermi level. Herein, the quantity and effective mass of electrons involved in thermal conduction are used to express thermal conductivity [46]:
(2)
λ =
π 2
N f
K B 2
T τ
3
m ∗
where λ is thermal conductivity, N
f is the number of free electrons per volume, K
B is Boltzmann constant, T is absolute temperature, τ is relaxation time, m* is effective mass, which indicates that the K is positively correlated with N
f and T. From Equation (2), the thermal conductivity shows a positive relationship with the number of free electrons per volume and absolute temperature.

When the temperature rises to a certain level, the electronic jump frequency that passes through the structural defects in the composite material begins to saturate, and the thermal component dominates the thermal conductivity of the composite material. Therefore, the electrons involved in thermal conductivity are scattered by various thermally excited elastic waves (called phonons), and the relaxation time between their collisions with each other is also shortened [47]. In short, when the temperature exceeds 448 K, the thermal conductivity of the composite material will have a negative correlation with the temperature. This is mainly because of the scattering of phonons and blocking of the electrons. For pure copper, this phenomenon is more noticeable.

In addition, it is worth noting that the thermal conductivity of the composites increases significantly, because the rich surface functional groups and GR-like core structure of the CPD gives it more metal binding sites and excellent electron transfer properties [48–50]. By the way, it is well known that the important factor to improve the efficiency of interface heat conduction is interface heat conduction [51,52]. For the CPD/Cu composite, the volume fraction, structure, radius and existing state of the CPD clusters have an imperative effect on interfacial thermal conductance. For example, the clean interface and grain boundary area changed by CPD clusters plays a critical role in enhancing the interfacial bonding and improving the thermal conductivity of CPD/Cu composites [53–55].

The black wireframe in Fig. 6(b) is enlarged in Fig. 6(c), a dense network structure with dispersed graphene dots is formed by the aggregation of the CPD. According to the microstructural analysis, the CPD not only has a high-purity graphite structure (sp2 bonding), but also contains a large amount of amorphous carbon structure (disordered mixture of sp2 and sp3), the intrinsically disordered structure in the amorphous carbon network leads to a continuous network. Schematic diagrams of interfacial structure between Cu and CPD as shown in Fig. 6(d), transition region is formed between the Cu (111) crystal plane of the matrix and the CPD (100) crystal plane of the reinforcement phase, making the interface bond more tightly. The thermal conductivity can be increased due to the reduction of the electron scatter [56]. Excellent interface bonding can provide a channel for heat transfer between electrons and phonons [57]. Besides, different from the results reported in the literature [7,58,59], Cu2O which has a significant blocking effect on the transmission of electrons and phonons is not found at the interface between copper and carbon. Therefore, amorphous carbon in the CPD may purify the grain boundary by eliminating of the oxygen atoms during the sintering, thus improving the thermal conductivity of the composites.

In this research, CPD/Cu composites with good mechanical and thermal properties were prepared via MLM and SPS process. CPD clusters are well dispersed at the Cu grain interfaces, and the clusters are demonstrated as an AC-network with lots of graphene dots in it. A good wettability and compact interface can be achieved by the formation of the transition region (~5 nm) due to the amorphous activated carbon at the edge of the AC-network. These microstructural characteristics can improve the mechanical and thermal properties of the CPD/Cu composite simultaneously. The discussed strengthening mechanism indicates that the reduction of defects and the purification of grain boundaries are important to promote the performance improvement.

Wenmin Zhao: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Rui Bao: Writing - review & editing. Jianhong Yi: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Dong Fang: Resources. Caiju Li: Investigation. Jingmei Tao: Validation. Fengxian Li: Software. Yichun Liu: Visualization. Xin You: Project administration. Songlin Tan: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Chinese National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 52064032), the Yunnan science and technology projects Grants (Grant No. 2019ZE001, No. 202002AB080001), Yunnan Ten Thousand Talents Plan Young & Elite Talents Project (YNWR-QNBJ-2018-005). Project also supported by State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, China.
